meta.title: 10.3 Back-Pressure and Flow Control
meta.description: When downstream systems are overwhelmed: back-pressure, load shedding, and graceful degradation.

blocks.prompt: Your message queue has 10 million messages waiting. Consumers process 1,000/second. At this rate, it takes 2.8 hours to clear the backlog. Meanwhile, new messages keep arriving. What do you do?

blocks.content:
## Back-pressure

When a system cannot keep up, it signals upstream to slow down. Like water pressure building up in a pipe — if the pipe is blocked, pressure pushes back.

**Strategies:**
- **Reject new work:** Return HTTP 503 (Service Unavailable) or 429. The fastest way to shed load.
- **Queue with limits:** Accept work into a bounded queue. When full, reject new requests.
- **Slow down producers:** Consumer signals producer to reduce rate (TCP flow control does this naturally).

## Load shedding

Deliberately drop requests to protect the system. Prioritize important traffic.

**Priority-based shedding:**
1. Shed analytics and logging first
2. Shed non-critical features (recommendations, social features)
3. Protect core features last (authentication, checkout, data safety)

**Cost-based shedding:** Estimate the cost of each request. Shed expensive requests first (complex queries, large file uploads).

## Graceful degradation

Return a reduced but functional response instead of an error.

| Component down | Degraded behavior |
|---|---|
| Recommendation engine | Show popular items |
| Search service | Show category browsing |
| User avatars | Show initials/default avatar |
| Real-time prices | Show cached prices with disclaimer |
| Reviews service | Hide reviews section |

Back-pressure signals upstream systems to slow down. Load shedding drops low-priority requests to protect critical ones. Graceful degradation returns reduced functionality instead of errors. All three are essential for systems that survive overload.

blocks.prompt: Module 10.4 covers observability — seeing what is happening inside your system so you can detect and respond to problems.

reviewQuestions.question: What is back-pressure in a distributed system?
options: A way to speed up slow producers
A signal to upstream systems to slow down when the system is overloaded
A caching strategy for high traffic
reviewQuestions.explanation: Back-pressure signals upstream systems to slow down when a downstream system cannot keep up, preventing overload.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right! Back-pressure prevents cascading failures by slowing things down before they break.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Back-pressure is when an overloaded system signals upstream to slow down — like water pressure building up in a blocked pipe.

reviewQuestions.question: What is load shedding?
options: Deliberately dropping low-priority requests to protect critical ones
Adding more servers during peak traffic
reviewQuestions.explanation: Load shedding deliberately drops requests to protect the system, prioritizing important traffic over less critical requests.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly! Sometimes dropping some requests is better than failing on all of them.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Load shedding means deliberately dropping low-priority requests to protect the system and keep critical traffic flowing.
